coredata.dc:title: Nanomaterials-enriched sensors for detection of chiral pharmaceuticals

coredata.dc:description: Advancements in nanoscience and nanotechnology have opened new pathways to fabricate novel nanostructures with interesting properties that would be used for different applications. In this respect, nanostructures comprising chirality are one of the most rapidly developing research fields encompassing chemistry, physics and biology. Chirality, also known as mirror asymmetry, describes the geometrical property of an object that is not superimposable on its mirror image. This characteristic plays a crucial role because these identical forms of chiral species in pharmaceuticals or food additives may exhibit different effects on living organisms. Therefore, chiral analysis is an important field of modern chemical analysis in health-related industries that are reliant on the production of enantiomeric compounds involving pharmaceuticals. This review covers the recent advances dealing with the synthesis, design and advantageous analytical performance of nanomaterials-enriched sensors used for chiral pharmaceuticals. We conclude this review with the challenges existing in this research field and our perspectives on some potential strategies with cutting-edge approaches for the rational design of sensors for chiral pharmaceuticals. We expect this comprehensive review will inspire future studies in nanomaterials-enriched chiral sensors.

head.ce:title: Nanomaterials-enriched sensors for detection of chiral pharmaceuticals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Advancements in nanoscience and nanotechnology have opened new pathways to fabricate novel nanostructures with interesting properties that would be used for different applications. In this respect, nanostructures comprising chirality are one of the most rapidly developing research fields encompassing chemistry, physics and biology. Chirality, also known as mirror asymmetry, describes the geometrical property of an object that is not superimposable on its mirror image. This characteristic plays a crucial role because these identical forms of chiral species in pharmaceuticals or food additives may exhibit different effects on living organisms. Therefore, chiral analysis is an important field of modern chemical analysis in health-related industries that are reliant on the production of enantiomeric compounds involving pharmaceuticals. This review covers the recent advances dealing with the synthesis, design and advantageous analytical performance of nanomaterials-enriched sensors used for chiral pharmaceuticals. We conclude this review with the challenges existing in this research field and our perspectives on some potential strategies with cutting-edge approaches for the rational design of sensors for chiral pharmaceuticals. We expect this comprehensive review will inspire future studies in nanomaterials-enriched chiral sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is a prominent property of natural systems and is essential in chemical biology and pharmacology [1]. Research on chiral molecules containing asymmetric carbon is of great importance in terms of understanding the biochemical processes and the origin of living organisms [2]. Accordingly, approaches used to distinguish different chiral forms of a chiral compound known as enantiomers that are non-superimposable mirror images of a compound are essential. However, two enantiomers of a chiral molecule exhibit an identical behavior in most of the chemical and physical properties and therefore they are often difficult to separate. Especially in a chiral environment, one enantiomer may produce the desired effect, while the other may become ineffective, or can produce serious side effects [3,4].

Chirality is of increasing interest in the pharmaceutical industry. This interest is due to differences in the pharmacological, toxicological, or metabolic activities of enantiomeric drugs [5,6]. It is reported that more than 50 % of currently used pharmaceuticals are chiral [7]. In addition, there is a lack of information on the chirality status (either racemates, single active enantiomer or achiral) of medicines circulated on the market. A recent study [8] has established the detailed chirality status of registered human medicines by manufacturers from 45 countries circulating on the market in Tanzania. Chiral drugs account for approximately 60 % of the total of 3573 registered human drugs. On the other hand, 74 % of registered chiral drugs have a racemate and the rest have a single enantiomer. In addition, a survey of new agrochemicals (44 commercial products) used as modern plant protection pesticides was conducted by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and found that 43 % of them were chiral products [9]. In chiral drugs, chirality originates from axis, plane, or asymmetry centers, but the important thing is that the carbon atom is the dominant stereogenic center. In most chiral pharmaceuticals, only one enantiomer provides pharmacological or biological activity, while the other form is toxic and does not show the desired effect or is less effective [10]. Therefore, enantiomers have different pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties [11]. Although there are several pharmaceutical drugs commercialized in both racemic and single enantiomers, single enantiomer forms have recently been preferred. Pharmaceuticals used in this way are S-citalopram (S-CIT), S-ketoprofen (S-KP), S-ibuprofen (S-IBU), S-omeprazole (S-OMEP) and S-ofloxacin (S-OFX) [11–13].

An increase in the global human population, advancements in healthcare strategies, investments in research, and aging societies in industrialized countries have led to a substantial increase in the consumption of pharmaceuticals [14]. Most synthetic pharmaceuticals are prepared in a racemic form. Therefore, the determination of enantiomeric excess (ee: measurement of purity used for chiral substances) is extremely important to reach the full therapeutic potential of these compounds [15]. Besides, due to the different physiological and therapeutic properties of their enantiomers, chemical analysis of chiral compounds has been an area of interest for researchers [16–18]. The techniques used for the analysis of these enantiomeric compounds include HPLC [19], capillary electrophoresis [20], GC [21] circular dichroism [22], spectrofluorometry [23], mass spectrometry [24], NMR [25], near-infrared spectrometry [26] and phosphorescence [27]. The validity of separation-based techniques depends on the performance of the separation steps. Moreover, some of these techniques are expensive and not easily accessible. Chirooptic methods can provide enantiodiscrimination quickly without the need for separation steps. However, the low sensitivity of these methods can be shown as a shortcoming [16,28]. Nanomaterials-enriched sensors are interesting and can be utilized as an alternative method that has recently received considerable attention of researchers, especially in optical methods. This is due to the good size-dependent optical properties of these particles. The use of optical nanoparticles is quite common in biomedical applications [29], biosensor applications [30], optical sensor applications [31,32], and biomarkers [33,34].

Chirality is not limited to molecular systems but may appear on all scales, ranging from spiral galaxies to nanoscale structures [35]. Nanoparticles (NPs) are composed of several atoms that are of the same order of magnitude as that of proteins and thus, must be able to also exhibit chirality [36]. Small metallic particles lead to the non-propagating confinement of light and to localized surface plasmon resonances, which create strong electric and magnetic dipoles located on the nanoparticles. A chiral particle (or a chiral assembly of interacting non-chiral particles) can be described as a coupled system of induced electric and magnetic dipole moments, which interact differently with right- and left-handed circularly polarized light. Indeed, the shape of the particle/assembly dictates whether a plasmon mode can be excited more efficiently by one circular polarization state compared to the other one [35]. For a long time, NPs have been pictured and described as simple spherical (or other symmetrical) objects, but at the atomic scale and even larger scales, they have complex features that display mirror asymmetry [37]. Chiral metallic nanoparticles have attracted the attention of researchers in the last decade [38]. Particles of gold, silver, platinum, and other noble metals have been reported using chiral ligands [39,40]. This field is particularly attractive because chirality is a basic property in biological systems. Therefore, the development of chiral nanomaterials, such as quantum dots, metallic nanoparticles, carbon-based nanomaterials and nanocomposites, is an important issue in the chirality-based research field. For the fabrication processes, in general, two approaches have been proposed. One is the top-down approach involving the local elimination of material from a larger object or film to obtain the desired shape, layout and properties [35]. A rather simpler approach to provide chirality in an inorganic nanoparticle is to coat its surface with optically active moieties by performing surface engineering or ligand attachment [41]. The other method is bottom-up that involves arranging small particles into more sophisticated hierarchical structures and assemblies [35]. In the respect of this review, chiral nanomaterials for chiral sensing applications have been mostly obtained by wet chemistry, including the coupling of chiral molecules with non-chiral plasmonic particles up to date.

In this review, considering the importance of chiral pharmaceuticals, we discuss the details of the recent applications using nanomaterials for the detection of chiral pharmaceuticals. The study was conducted by examining nanomaterials in several subclasses and collecting the studies reported in the literature under different subheadings. In this respect, the Web of Science (WOS) search of total chiral sensor studies and nanomaterials-enriched chiral sensor studies for chiral pharmaceuticals reported between 2010 and 2022 is expressed in Fig. 1. Looking at the graph, it is seen that the interest of researchers in chiral sensors is increasing by the year. However, despite the importance of chirality in pharmaceuticals and the advance in nanomaterials, it is seen that the number of studies conducted with nanomaterials-enriched sensors for chiral pharmaceuticals remains stable. In addition, many studies relevant to chiral sensor applications were published in the literature [39,40,42–44]. However, there is no study reviewing the detection of chiral pharmaceuticals using nanomaterials. Therefore, this review will help researchers who will work in the field of developing sensors for chiral pharmaceuticals.

Colorimetric sensors are widely used because of their advantages such as low cost and identifying the results with the naked eye without the need for tools and equipment [45,46]. In chiral sensor studies, it is a great advantage that a molecular recognition event that can visually distinguish chiral structures depends on color change [47]. Today, metal nanoparticle-based colorimetric sensors are used in the detection of many complex structures, such as the detection of carbohydrates [48], amino acids [49] and proteins [50]. The targeted analyte can be detected by the color change that occurs by causing reactions such as dispersion/aggregation of metal nanoparticles [51–53]. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are frequently used nanomaterials in the colorimetric analysis due to their physicochemical properties. These nanoparticles are small and have a large surface area and specific optical properties [46,54–56]. The unique optical properties of AuNPs and AgNPs are strongly influenced by their inter-particle distance. Once the average distance between NPs becomes considerably small, NPs aggregate and a red shift occurs in their localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) band, which is accompanied by a visible color change, e.g. from red to blue for AuNPs and from yellow to brown for AgNPs [57]. However, there are rare studies in the literature for detection of nanoparticle-based chiral pharmaceuticals.

The enantiospecific interactions with chiral NPs can be used as the basis for separations and detection methods. The local surface structures of the NPs can be chiral, and can influence the enantioselectivity. However, the inhomogeneous distribution of different surface orientations averages their influence on enantioselectivity to zero. By the advance of using single crystalline NPs, the optical polarimetry technique can be used to detect chiral pharmaceuticals. In addition, the use of chiral nanoparticles combined with a density separation method such as centrifugation can be used for the enantiomeric purification of real pharmaceuticals. Enantioselective discrimination of the chiral pharmaceutical propranolol (PLL) was reported in the solution phase using tetrahexahedral (THH, 24-sided) AuNPs modified with D- or L -cysteine (Cys) to realize the chiral recognition of S-PLL and R-PLL [15]. Polarimetry was used to measure the spin of polarized light that varies linearly with the enantiomeric excesses of each of the solutions containing mixtures of PLL and Cys/THH@AuNPs. Binding of R- or S-PLL from aqueous solution to the chiral-modified surfaces (D- and L -Cys/THH@AuNPs) was enantiospecific, which was used as the basis for chromatography-based separation methods or centrifugation.

It has been reported that some metal nanomaterials containing Pt, Au, Pd, and Ru are used in applications of hybrid structures formed by decorating with graphite-like carbon nitride nanosheets (g-C3N4 NHs) [58–62]. The use of graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has become widespread in many areas such as catalysis, degradation, and sensors [63–67]. The low solubility of solid g-C3N4 in water creates a disadvantage in its use in applications. However, it has been observed that g-C3N4 NHs synthesized by ultrasonication-assisted liquid exfoliation have high water dispersibility, biocompatibility, and higher photocatalytic properties [68,69]. The g-C3N4 nanosheets functionalized with AuNPs can pair with nucleic acids or proteins with Au-S or Au-N bonds [70,71] and it can be used as a suitable material for producing biosensors that can amplify the ECL signal [59,72,73]. It is advantageous to use Hb as the chiral selector and Au-g-C3N4 NHs as the luminophore. In addition, it is important note herein that the steric configuration of protein molecules have different effect on affinity for configuration of chiral molecules due to the shape fitting and non-covalent interactions such as hydrogen bond, hydrophobic interaction and electrostatic attraction. A composite structure (g-C3N4 NHs functionalized with hemoglobin (Hb) and AuNPs) was developed for the specific recognition of penicillamine (PEN) enantiomers using a new stable and stereoselective electrochemiluminescence (ECL) interface [73]. D-PEN can be used to treat Wilson's disease, hepatitis and rheumatoid arthritis and is used to prevent retinopathy of prematurity in premature infants [74–76] while L-PEN shows toxic effects that cause some harmful reactions such as neuritis and osteomyelitis [73,77]. It was observed that the developed ECL chiral sensor operates in the range of 1.0 × 10−4 M- 5.0 × 10−3 M in the detection of PEN enantiomers. The limit of detection (LOD) of the L-PEN and D-PEN were found to be 3.1 × 10−5 M and 3.3 × 10−5 M respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the reproducibility of the prepared sensor was found to be 4.2 % with high selectivity. To prove applicability for real samples, the designed interface was applied to determine the content of D-PEN in the tablet by the standard addition method. The recovery rates were found between 95.5 % and 102.7 % with the RSD between 2.9 % and 4.0 %, which demonstrates the satisfying feasibility of the sensor for real samples [73].

Nanomaterial-enriched colorimetric sensors are frequently used in the detection mechanism of DNA, proteins, and many small molecules [46,54,55,82–84]. Nanoparticles used in colorimetric sensors can provide visual chiral recognition in the presence of only one enantiomer by clustering rapidly in enantioselective identification [56]. As chiral selectors, aptamers have great advantages in their ability to recognize specific target molecules, easy immobilization, and stability at different temperatures and a wide pH range. They can show a stereoselective binding affinity for chiral drugs to discriminate the enantiomers with very high enantioselectivity. Combination of this stereoselective binding affinity with visual color change is highly desirable because a molecular recognition event is transduced into an appreciable color change. In addition, this kind of qualitative results can be also converted to quantitative data by smartphone-based applications, which are popular throughout the world providing new opportunities for on-site applications including pharmaceutical analysis and environmental monitoring. As an interesting and prominent study by Ping et al. [79], a smartphone-based color analysis has been carried out to colorimetric enantiodiscrimination of the IBU enantiomers. IBU is one of the nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs [85]. Due to its chirality, S-IBU is known to be almost entirely responsible for the anti-inflammatory effects of rac-IBU [79,86]. In the study, aptamer (Apt) coated S-Apt/R-Apt@AuNPs were used to detect enantiomeric structures. The performed application analysis process is shown in Fig. 2A-F. To collect the calibration points, the user chose an area using the on-screen “construct new sensor”. Then the test is named and the user chooses “Load picture” or “Take picture”. Following this step, to characterize the sample’s color, the application locates the reference colors (100 pixels), and calculates an average red, green, and blue (RGB) value. It automatically converts the exponential RGB values to linear RGB values. Then, the data is saved and the application calculates the concentration according to calibration curve. To support the enantioselective mechanism, the absorption spectrum of the Apt-capped AuNPs solution responding to S-IBU or R-IBU is recorded (Figs. 2G and 2H). The LOD value was found as 1.24 pg/mL for S-IBU and 3.91 pg/mL for R-IBU. In real samples, the recovery values were calculated as 93–100 % and 98–105 % for S-IBU and R-IBU, respectively. This result indicates that the designed sensor demonstrates its reliability for colorimetric detection of IBU in real sample applications. We foresee that the approach may contribute to the development of applications known as on-site determination in the chiral recognition of pharmaceuticals using the smartphone application [79].

AgNPs are seen as one of the ideal nanoparticles in colorimetric sensor applications. The reason is that they show high extinction properties with ideal colors [87]. In addition, the ease of their surface modification enables to build of selective surfaces. Cysteine has a strong affinity for adsorption onto metal surfaces including AgNPs, leading to the formation of self-assembled monolayers (SAMs). The SAMs provide an increase in the selectivity of the modified surface and function as intermediates for further reaction or complexation with other molecules. Taking advantage of this property in this context, a colorimetric sensor was designed for the discrimination of ketoprofen (KP) enantiomers using L -cysteine coated silver nanoparticles ( L -Cys@AgNPs) [56]. KP is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug that has R(-) and S(+) enantiomers. It can control factors that cause pain or inflammation in many diseases in the body [88–90]. S-KP is approximately a thousand times more efficient than R-KP [56]. In the used approach, no color change was observed in solution color and absorption peak upon addition of S-ketoprofen which showed that there was no selective response towards S-KP. In addition, no significant changes were observed in the absorption peak and solution color of L -Cys@AgNPs with R-KP in the absence of NaCl. However, a color change was observed in the solution with the aggregation caused by the addition of R-KP and NaCl to L -Cys@AgNPs. The color of the solution, which was yellow, turned green in the presence of R-KP indicating the enantioselective interaction of R-KP with L -Cys@AgNPs (see Fig. 3.). LOD value was calculated as 4.52 μM in the studied concentration range of 8.33–33.3 μM. The recovery percentage of the designed sensor was found between 100.02 % and 109.15 % in the tested real samples. These values are applicable for the sensitive determination of KP enantiomers in environmental water samples. In addition, this study is the first application of a colorimetric sensor study for KP enantiomers using L -Cys@AgNPs.

Besides AuNPs and AgNPs, metal oxides can be alternative nanoparticles for the preparation of electrochemical sensors to detect chiral pharmaceuticals. Metal oxides are well-known materials for sensor applications. In fact, the surface of metal oxides plays a key role in effective interaction with target molecules. Tailoring the surface properties of metal oxide allows for improving sensitive properties. Vancomycin-functionalized zinc oxide was modified on indium tin oxide (ITO) glass substrate (ZnO/Van/ITO) and a new chiral sensor was developed for the recognition of PEN enantiomers [81]. The LOD values of the electrochemical chiral sensor were 23.56 mM for the D-PEN and 14.73 mM for the L-PEN. The obtained electrode showed high performance in terms of low sensitivity, and a wide linearity range for PEN enantiomers.

As a conclusion to this section of the review, Table 1 shows chiral sensor studies using metallic nanoparticles in the literature. As seen, metallic nanoparticles-enriched chiral sensors have been successfully applied for sensing chiral pharmaceuticals. However, it should be noted that only AuNPs and AgNPs have been widely used, especially in optical-based chiral sensors. This can be attributed to their intense colors which may tend to change due to the aggregation as the result of selective interaction. Also, the methodology for the production of chiral surfaces only involves the surface modification of metallic nanoparticles. In addition, researchers have usually prepared various electrochemical chiral sensing interfaces based on Cys.

Magnetic particles in a solid form that respond to magnetism can be in the form of single nanoparticles or clusters of micro and nanoparticles. Each nanoparticle has a specific use. The synthesis pathway composition and size of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) differ in applications. Superparamagnetic, ferro and ferri particles are used in drug delivery applications. Such materials are affected by the external magnetic field. This is due to the magnetic moment of the mesh unit and the structure of its domains. In the absence of an external magnetic field, they behave like inactive particles [91]. Appropriate recognition units modified with MNPs are homogeneously dispersed in the sample, which can specifically capture the desired target and are collected via the magnetic field. After the resulting analyte-MNPs structures are brought to the detection surface, they can be isolated or removed from the sample. In addition, MNPs not only detect the desired analyte but also can act as part of a transducer in a sensor [91,92]. Thanks to their strong magnetic characteristics that biological systems do not possess, MNPs are one of the key labels in biosensors. Therefore, this unique behavior allows MNPs to build advanced platforms for high-performance sensors in biomedical applications [91]. To use the advantages of MNPs, a magnetic chiral discrimination approach was explored by combining the enantioselectivity of bovine serum albumin (BSA) with crystallization resolution to produce optically pure drug substances containing the racemic compound IBU and OFX [93]. For this purpose, magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles coated with chiral selective BSA were used. BSA was immobilized on superparamagnetic iron oxides by electrostatic adsorption. Since chiral drugs have special separation sites, the selective binding properties of BSA were used to separate the enantiomer from the racemic compound. MNPs used in multi-step processes served to optimize partially dissolved mixtures with high enantiomeric properties when functionalized with BSA. The hybrid system was obtained by functionalizing an enantiomer with magnetically immobilized BSA and crystallization was used to produce an enantiomer of higher optical purity.

Chirooptic methods (i.e. optical rotary dispersion and circular dichroism) can perform fast enantiodiscrimination without the need for separation. However, in these methods, it is necessary to pay attention to parameters such as low sensitivity [16]. To overcome this problem, there have been studies on different approaches. To increase and control the sensitivity for enantiomers of NAP and phenyl glycine (PHY) as model compounds, a method was suggested based on different interactions between the model chiral enantiomers and magnetite chiral nanospheres modified with D -tryptophan and L -tryptophan [28]. The different interactions of the chiral nanospheres and the model enantiomers lead to a significant change in the resonance light scattering (RLS) behaviors of nanospheres. Chiral magnetite nanospheres, namely D-(+)-tryptophan modified magnetite nanospheres (+MNSs) and L-(-)-tryptophan modified magnetite nanospheres (−MNSs) play a critical role in acting as a probe for the identification of model enantiomers. +MNSs are very sensitive to (R)-(−)-NAP and D-(−)-α-phenyl glycine, while –MNSs are very sensitive to (R)-(−)−NAP, L-(+)-α-phenyl glycine (+PHY). The greatly enhanced RLS signal of magnetite chiral nanospheres in the presence of these enantiomers has provided a sensitive analysis of the model chiral compounds as low as ng/L levels. In addition, the magnetic properties of chiral nanospheres modified with tryptophan enantiomers can provide a methodologic approach for the enantiomeric ratio analysis without using any separation step, which leads to simpler, more sensitive and cost-effective analysis than conventional techniques (e.g. GC, HPLC, electrophoresis, NMR, phosphorescence).

The efficient use of quantum dots (QDs) or nanocrystalline semiconductors in optical sensors has recently emerged. The fluorescent properties of QDs are the main reason for their use in detection techniques [94]. QD surfaces can be used for different sensor designs using suitable materials [95–100]. The fluorescent properties of QDs can be changed by modifications made by using stereospecific chiral molecules as capping molecules on the surface of QDs, as well as providing a new perspective for chemical sensors [101]. Recent advances in the design and preparation of new chiral materials have assisted the development of molecular recognition methods [102]. They have a wide usage area in many fields, such as spintronics [103], chemical and biological sensing [104,105], photonics [106], cell imaging [107], asymmetric catalysis [108], enantio-recognition [42].

The use of QDs in sensor studies has increased [109–112], however, their use as optical probes for enantioselective recognition has just begun [113–116]. Core-shell (CS) cadmium selenide/zinc sulfide (CdSe/ZnS-QDs) have important properties such as high surface-to-volume ratio, high surface energy and fluorescence. Their unique properties enable these materials to be used in different applications [117,118]. With the work done by Delgado-Pérez et al. [80], a chiral sensor study was carried out based on CdSe/ZnS CS-QDs. Chiral QDs were synthesized by treating amine-capped QDs with N-acetyl- L -cysteine methyl ester differing in core or shell size, and optical properties. The QDs exhibit significant stability as well as significant chiral separation of the APA enantiomers. In another study performed with chiral QDs with a different transducing approach, a chemical sensor was developed based on the measurement of the circular dichroism (CD) signal generated from cysteamine-capped cadmium sulfide quantum dots (Cys@CdS-QDs) [34]. The potential of this sensor was evaluated using D-PEN and L-PEN chiral-thiol molecules. PEN, a sulfur-containing amino acid, is a pharmaceutically essential chiral compound that is a degradation product of penicillin [119,120]. Although D-PEN is the pharmaceutical form, L-PEN has a toxic effect [121]. It has been expected that these compounds giving little CD signal can improve the signals using Cys@CdS-QDs. LOD values were found to be 0.49 µM for D-PEN and 0.74 µM for L-PEN. For the control of the proposed sensor, excellent recoveries in the range of 94–106 % were obtained as a result of the detection of PEN in the urine sample with an RSD value of less than 6.0 % [34].

Chemical functionalization of a surface with Cys was applied for CdSe-QDs, and an optical nanosensor was developed for chiral recognition of L-Morphine (L-MOR) [122] (see Fig. 4). In the approach, the fluorescent intensities of L- and D -cysteine-functionalized CdSe-QDs were quenched by adding D-methamphetamine and L-MOR, respectively. However, the interaction between D -cysteine-functionalized CdSe-QDs and L-MOR is more efficient and exhibits more fluorescence quenching with a LOD value of 0.06 μM.

In recent years, carbon-based QDs (CQDs) have been widely used in optical sensor applications. Of these, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) attract attention due to their low toxicity and good optical properties [125,126]. GQDs are a class of zero-dimensional graphene sheets. By carving two-dimensional graphene layers into zero-dimensional GQDs (smaller than 100 nm), they exhibit exciton confinement and quantum-size effect [127,128]. These materials combine the advantages of graphene and carbon dots [129] and offer the opportunity to be used in different fields, especially fluorescence. GQDs show unique fluorescence properties. These properties are due to quantum confinement and edge effects [130]. GQDs can successfully identify analytical targets including metal ions [131,132], anions [133,134], and small molecules [135,136]. The precursor materials for the preparation of CQDs generally establish them rich with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which provide the ability to bind with metal ions. This interaction results in fluorescence quenching which can be recovered by removing the metal ions with much more strong interaction (off-on switch). In this context, a metal-induced fluorescence quenching-based fluorescence sensor based on GQDs was designed using lead ions for detecting D-PEN [123]. As shown with the schematic representation of the mechanism in Fig. 5, the fluorescence emission was quenched by Pb2+ through the electron transfer process. The addition of D-PEN induced the competitive combination owing to the strong coordination between D-PEN and Pb2+, which resulted in the fluorescence recovery and the fluorescent switch for D-PEN detection. The real sample study performed in human urine sample exhibited a low LOD value of 0.47 μM, and good recoveries between 96.84 % and 102.13 %. This approach can be extended to developing paper-based sensors for on-site detection of D-PEN.

Table 2 shows chiral sensor studies for pharmaceuticals using QDs in the literature. It is seen that generally small chiral molecules are used for surface functionalization as receptor units most probably because of the small size of QDs. Additionally, fluorescence spectroscopy is one of the most preferred techniques since photoluminescence is the widely used characteristic of QDs. There is no on-site detection application for pharmaceuticals performed by QDs. We foresee that chiral QDs will be used to design paper/polymer-based sensors for the detection of chiral pharmaceuticals.

The effective use of nanomaterials in different fields recently has led to an increased interest in these materials [137,138]. In addition combination of different types of nanomaterials may allow a synergetic effect in a sensing process. Most chiral selectors are not conductive except for some conductive chiral ionic liquids. In the case of a chiral selector being modified on an electrode to construct an electrochemical chiral sensor, its poor conductivity will hinder the transmission of electrochemical signals. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are widely used in electrochemical sensor applications due to their good electrical conductivity, excellent chemical stability, large aspect ratio, and high mechanical strength and stiffness. A CNTs-enriched enantioselective and sensitive electrochemical immunosensor was designed for the detection of chiral antibiotic OFX [78]. OFX is a broad-spectrum antibiotic widely used against many bacteria and some anaerobes in bacterial infections in medicine and veterinary applications [139]. Although OFX exists as a racemate of the R- and S-enantiomers, the therapeutic efficacy of S-enantiomer to various drugs is more effective, especially in infectious diseases [140]. The antibacterial activity of S-OFX is approximately 10–100 times better than that of its enantiomer, R-OFX. Also, in terms of antibacterial activity, the efficacy of S-OFX is approximately twice higher than that of the racemic OFX [141]. The basic principle of the sensor relied on a dual amplification strategy using multi-walled carbon nanotubes-poly( L -lysine) as a matrix to immobilize antigen and multi-enzyme-antibody functionalized gold nanoflowers as an electrochemical sensing tag. As the results of the study, the working range of the immunosensor was between 0.26 and 25.6 ng/mL for S-OFX and between 0.37 and 12.8 ng/mL for R-OFX. The LOD values for S-OFX and R-OFX were found to be 0.15 ng/mL and 0.30 ng/mL, respectively. The recovery values for S-OFX immunosensor is found as 93.5–108.2 % and 94.5–106.5 % for R-OFX immunosensor. The immunosensor can be designed to be used in different applications for detecting pharmaceuticals and pollutants in different fields such as food and environmental analysis.

In another study conducted with CNTs [142], the effect of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) was investigated in the chiral separation of twelve pharmaceutical racemate classes. Within these twelve groups, blockers, anti-inflammatory drugs, antifungal drugs, dopamine antagonists, norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitors, catecholamines, sedative-hypnotics, diuretics, antihistamines, anticancer drugs, and antiarrhythmic drugs were investigated. To understand the properties of SWCNTs used in chiral separation, multimodal elution was performed. For this, reverse phase, polar organic and normal phase chromatographic conditions were used using nanoliquid chromatography. Under the aforementioned conditions, fundamental separation results were obtained for the pharmaceuticals celiprolol, chlorpheniramine, etozoline, nomifensine, and sulconazole. As a work output, SWCNTs represent a promising stationary phase for chiral separation.

Graphene, often called “miracle material” for its outstanding characteristics, is a two-dimensional material composed of an atomic sheet of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms patterned in a honeycomb lattice [143]. Graphene possesses unique features extremely sensitive to the environment. Therefore, it is natural to consider employing graphene for sensors [144]. The large surface area of graphene makes it easier to modify surfaces with desired recognition unit, and its zero bandgap is beneficial for accelerating electron transfer between the target analyte and the electrode surface. Given their advantageous properties and functionalities, graphene-based materials (GBMs) are under active research, and researchers have exerted a great effort in the fabrication of graphene-based sensors. GBMs are convenient materials for sensors because they offer smaller, lighter and endless design possibilities to the research and technology community. To form stable suspensions, graphene layers must be stabilized by molecules or supramolecules through either chemical or physical functionalization [145]. In this respect, an electrochemical chiral sensor was developed for detecting PLL enantiomers using a carbon paste electrode (CPE) modified with uracil supramolecular structures [124]. The convenient conditions for creating a chiral selective unit on the surface of graphene were examined by molecular dynamics simulation and voltammetry. In molecular dynamics simulation calculation, it was found that the adsorption energy of R-PLL on graphene was close to that of S-PLL indicating that the profiles of changes in free energy of the interaction of enantiomers with the graphene surface were almost identical for both enantiomers. Interestingly, after modification of CPE with uracil, S-PLL formed two hydrogen bonds but only one with R-PLL, which leads to stronger interaction than the R-PLL at the uracil-modified CPE surface.

Moxifloxacin (MOX) hydrochloride is a synthetic antibiotic chiral drug [146]. Upadhyay et al. [147] have proposed an electrochemical nanomaterial-enriched chiral sensor for MOX enantiomers. The authors have employed a CPE modified with β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD) as the chiral selector and graphene nanosheets (GNS) as nanomaterial. The preparation of β-CD@GNS and chiral recognition mechanism for the MOX enantiomers were shown in Fig. 6. The proposed electrochemical enantioselective sensor exhibited a highly efficient response for MOX sensing which was explained by the large peak potential shift difference of 122 mV for the enantiomers. This separation in the signal of MOX enantiomers could be attributed to the stereoselectivity of the chiral environment provided by β-CD. As a real sample analysis, MOX enantiomers were determined using this sensor in human urine and blood serum samples. The results of recovery tests performed in human urine and blood serum samples were found in the range of 97.43–99.60 % for all samples with an RSD of 3.1 %.

In the sensor field, studying functionalized forms of graphene usually begins with chemically oxidized graphene. The history of graphene oxide (GO) extends back many decades to some of the earliest research, including the chemistry of graphite [148,149]. GO possessing tunable oxygen functionalities on its basal planes facilitate the surface modification with the desired materials. Chemical modification of graphene oxide has been a promising route to achieving mass production of graphene-related materials and their use in a myriad of application fields. In the context of this review, Guo et al. [150] have designed an electrochemical chiral sensor for NAP enantiomers using BSA, Toluidin blue O (TBO), and reduced graphene oxide (rGO). They have reported that TBO@rGO nanosheets act as a support material for the BSA that is used as a chiral selector. Additionally, their results showed that the proposed sensor exhibits good electrochemical response and fast electron transfer under optimized experimental conditions. The chiral sensing response is based on the signal difference before and after the interaction of chiral interface with NAP enantiomers. A higher signal discrepancy is observed for R-NAP. In another study, Liu et al. [151] developed a differential potential ratiometric sensing platform with a composite membrane system composed of reduced graphene oxide and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP). PLL, an important β-adrenergic antagonist, is widely used in cardiovascular disorders [152–154]. In children, PLL is generally used for drug treatment of infantile migraine and hemangioma [155,156]. An enantiomer of the PLL, S-PLL has pharmacological performance for heart disease, while R-PLL has a membrane stabilizing or contraceptive effect. In addition, S-PLL provides 100 times higher activity than R-PLL [151,157,158]. Thanks to the hybrid system created in the study, effective specific detection of S-PLL was achieved by chitosan coupled MIP and signal amplification was performed using hydrophilic rGO nanomaterial. Under optimal experimental conditions, it was observed that the highest potential difference did not exceed 3.44 % in the measurement made one week later. The sensor system, and synergistic effect of rGO and MIP were shown in Fig. 7. The detection of PLL enantiomers is especially in demand in the pharmaceutical industry and clinical applications associated with heart disease. However, no commercial test kit has been produced yet.

Table 3 shows the carbon-based nanomaterials-enriched chiral sensor studies reported in the literature. As seen, generally cyclodextrin is used as a surface functional group in chiral pharmaceutical sensors. This can be attributed to many routes for ease of functionalization of carbon-based nanomaterials with cyclodextrin derivatives, which contributes to a chiral differentiation effect for enantiomers in the sensing approach. It should be also remarked that the most preferred technique is electrochemistry due to the versatility of electrochemical methods for the redox-active target materials.

Carbon materials are widely used in electrochemical detection systems. These include electrode performance materials such as graphene, CNTs and carbon nanofibers (CNFs) [147,163,164]. The use of CNFs in sensing systems is due to their specific surface area and high conductivity. Contrary to these properties, pure CNFs are disadvantageous due to their poor dispersibility in water. To overcome this negative aspect, CNFs can be encapsulated with polymers or functionalized with certain groups and combined with nanomaterials to obtain new structures [165,166]. This approach was applied to develop an electrochemical chiral sensor [159]. The working principle of this sensor is based on the competitive supramolecular interaction of sulfonyl ether-β-cyclodextrin (SBE-β-CD) CNFs embedded with optically active cationic drugs in a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). With this detection platform, recognition of R-/S-AML, R-/S-CBL and R-/S-MET enantiomers were investigated. It was the first attempt to apply SBE-β-CD for the recognition of AML/MET/CBL enantiomers and the chiral sensor proposed in the study was a promising approach for cationic enantiomers containing primary and secondary amino groups.

Metal-organic frameworks are multidimensional (one/two/three) porous materials composed of extensive metal mesh or atoms coordinated with polydentate ligands. Chiral metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are of increasing interest in the field of enantiomer separation and asymmetric catalysis [161,167,168]. As a chiral nanocomposite-based sensor, an electrochemical biomimetic sensor was designed using square wave voltammetry to enantioselectively separate ETB isomers [161]. ETB has three stereochemical configurations (RR,SS,RS). Among them, SS-ETB constitutes the active part, while RR-ETB has a toxic effect and RS-ETB is inactive. In addition, SS-ETB is 12 times more active than the other two isomers [169]. While SS-ETB has antibacterial activity, RR-ETB can cause optical blindness [170]. To discriminate these chiral species, β-cyclodextrin-based copper metal-organic framework (CD-CuMOF) was synthesized and modified on GCE. The chiral discrimination of ETB enantiomers was obtained based on the difference in oxidation peak potential of isomers (see Fig. 8). The RR-ETB enantiomer showed a linear response in the range of 1.0×10−7 to 1.0×10−4 M and a low LOD value of 3.10×10−8 M. For SS-ETB isomers, these values range from 5.0×10−7 to 2.5×10−4 M with a low LOD value of 8.52×10−8 M. The stability of the designed sensor was measured after 4 months. A very small change of 5 % was observed in the voltammetric signal. The detection of ETB isomers in racemic mixtures in real samples such as blood, urine and pharmaceutical was carried out with the designed sensor. CD-CuMOF can be considered as a low-cost and stable material that can offer an advantage for sensing and catalysis in the future.

MOFs have been also employed as enzyme biosensors due to the catalytic metal centers, large specific surface area, modifiable organic ligands and satisfying biocompatibility. Upon combining MOFs with enzymes in electrochemical sensing, considerable attention has been paid to the electroactivity of metal nodes and enzyme carriers, other than MOF’s porosity. The acetylcholinesterase (AChE) biosensors were constructed for the electrochemical detection of galantamine hydrobromide (GH) using isomorphic 3D chiral Ni-MOF with different pore sizes [162]. The combination of dense metal nodes with highly active AChE creates synergistic catalysis hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine chloride to produce a fast sensing response. The created sensor platform performed highly sensitive GH detection with the lowest LOD value of 0.31 pM. It showed a wide linear range of 1×10−12–1×10−6 M in the reported pharmaceutical AChE inhibitor detection systems. The prepared biosensor retained 88.53 % of its original response after 10 days, which shows that the short time storage stability of the biosensor is acceptable. The biosensor had good reproducibility with a good RSD value found to be 1.13 %. In the sensor application for GH detection in human serum samples, the recovery rate was found to be 97.00–105.22 %. As schematized in Fig. 9, these quantities are due to the size and chirality of the MOF pores significantly improving the host-guest interactions between the substrate and the AChE biosensor.

Solid contact ion-selective electrodes (SC-ISE), which are new generation potentiometric ion sensors, have shown a wider application area due to their significant advantages over traditional ISEs [171,172]. As ion-electron converters, SC-ISEs include various solid contact functional materials such as conductive polymers (CPs) [173,174] and nanomaterials [175,176]. Conductive polymers are the most used solid transducers in ISEs [177]. In addition to the advantages of these materials, there are disadvantages such as the formation of internal water films [174], sensitivity to light, oxygen, CO2 and instability of the pH range [175,176]. These problems have highlighted the use of carbon-based materials [160]. CNF, which is one of these materials, has great advantages in electroanalysis studies thanks to the stacked cones and plates in its structure with large edges [178]. In the field of electrochemical sensors, natural/modified cyclodextrins (α-CD, β-CD, γ-CD) are preferred worldwide due to their various properties [179–182]. In the study by Gadhari et al. [160], a potentiometric sensor was designed for the enantioselective recognition of RR- and SS-MOX (RR-MOX/SS-MOX) based on a chiral nanocomposite of sulfated γ-cyclodextrin (Sγ-CD). They developed a new SC-ISE using CNF incorporated in plasticized polyvinyl chloride (PVC). Dual center chirality in the biomolecule poses more potential hazards compared to single-center optical chiral compounds [183]. MOX, one pharmaceutical that is an example of these biomolecules, has two chiral centers. It has two pairs of enantiomers. Among these forms, SS-MOX active antibiotic, RR-MOX antimer, and SR-MOX/ RS-MOX forms are other impurities [146]. SS-MOX is a highly antibacterial form of the fluoroquinolone family used for the treatment of various infectious diseases such as bacterial sinusitis, pneumonia and chronic bronchitis [184]. In the presence of MOX, the distomer fragment (RR-MOX) may have an opposite toxic effect. The practical analytical utility of the developed chiral potentiometric sensor was demonstrated by SS-MOX measurement in pharmaceutical formulations, urine and blood serum samples. The linear range of the designed sensor was found to be between 1×10−2 and 6.5×10−7 M with a LOD value of 1.5 ± 0.16×10−7 M. The selectivity of the optimized procedure was tested on urine and blood serum samples. The recovery rate for all matrices was obtained in the range of 98–102 %, indicating that the procedure was highly selective.

In recent years, MIPs have been recognized as new synthetic receptors due to their outstanding advantages such as high stability, predetermined recognition ability, high mechanical strength, and low cost [185–187]. Gutiérrez-Climente et al. [185] have conducted a new potentiometric sensor study using chiral imprinted nanoparticles as the recognition element for S-CIT, which is an antidepressant used to treat anxiety and panic disorders [188,189]. A PVC membrane was used and potentiometric sensors were obtained by adding nanoparticles synthesized by precipitation polymerization to these membranes. Real sample analysis was studied in the urine sample of a voluntarily treated person with this drug. It was found that the sensor measures accurately and with precision (RSD= 2.07–6.56 %) for detecting S-CIT at different concentrations [185].

As a result, it is seen that composite nanomaterials can be alternatively used in the design of convenient chiral selectors. Besides, it is seen that there are limited studies relevant to chiral imprinted studies for the development of chiral sensors for pharmaceuticals in the literature.

In this review, we outline the considerable efforts that have been invested in the design and fabrication of nanomaterials-enriched sensors for detecting chiral pharmaceuticals over the last decade. Employing nanoparticles in this field provides advantages to gaining insight to understand the occurring selective interaction at the molecular level. For instance, nanoparticles, such as AuNPs and AgNPs with a chiral surface or functionalized with chiral linker molecules, can show a color change by forming chiral aggregates in the presence of one enantiomer of a chiral species. Due to their characteristics such as convenient size, chiral shape, optical response in the visible region and exceptionally strong circular polarization of photons, these NPs can even be used for intracellular sensing or behaving of chiral pharmaceuticals. It is important herein to remark that it is an advantage to functionalize surfaces of metal nanoparticles easily via a chiral unit, i.e. cysteine is the most common chiral species/linker in the functionalization of AuNPs and AgNPs surfaces. From the point of electrochemical sensing, nanomaterials can be utilized as an amplifier for the construction of electrochemical sensors. For instance, graphene provides outstanding characteristics, including a large specific surface area, prominent electrical conductivity and a wide electrochemical window in electrochemical applications. Besides its fast electron transfer rate role, it shows a difference in electrochemical response, such as redox peak potential difference, in a chiral interaction of enantiomers of a chiral species with functionalized graphene surface. Yet, there remain numerous challenges and areas for development to realize the full potential of chiral nanomaterials in chiral sensors. Chiral nanomaterials are relatively less explored than organic molecules in chiral relevant studies. Unlike chiral organic materials, an accurate understanding of the source of chirality in inorganic materials, including metallic or semiconductors, is still a challenge and in the development stage [41]. Most of the work on chiral nanomaterials has been devoted to noble metals, predominantly gold, increasing chiral plasmonic nanostructures. MIP nanomaterials with different forms, such as nanoparticles, core-shell/hollow nanoparticles, nanowires, nanotubes and nanofilms have been synthesized in a controlled way by the employment of nanotechnologies and surface chemistry [190]. Although nanosized metal-organic frameworks (nanoMOFs) have highly ordered porous architectures, well-defined pore size and a high density of chiral recognition sites that led to highly efficient separation capabilities for enantiomeric mixtures, there are rarely studies exploring their chiral sensing properties. The new knowledge and advancements in chiral nanomaterials will draw new routes to chiral sensors. This is because the methodology for the construction of a chiral surface for the aim of chiral sensing is no more than surface functionalization with a capping chiral agent. Therefore, from a future perspective, stable methods for large-scale synthesis of chiral nanomaterials are critical and needed in the field of chiral sensors for chiral pharmaceuticals. Especially, exploring the inherent chirality of nanomaterials may contribute to novelty not only for identifying the structural chiral properties of nanostructures but also for designing chiral sensors. Besides, the improvement in the current methods and the development of new ones for large-scale production in surface printing of nanomaterials may lead to the development of effective chiral sensors and to an understanding of the mechanism behind the interaction of chiral species, especially for chiral pharmaceuticals. Multifunctional magnetic chiral nanoparticles can be synthesized for enantioselective separation or preconcentration and sensing with color change. Combining optical or electrochemical properties with MIPs or MOFs can be suitable serve for these applications.

There are rare studies relevant to nanoparticle-based sensors in the literature for detecting chiral pharmaceuticals. More importantly, on-site approaches have not been focused on, and no study has been performed for enantioselective applications in the detection of chiral pharmaceuticals. From the technology readiness level (TRL) perspective, the scientific research for the detection of chiral pharmaceuticals is still beginning, which is between TRL 1 and TRL 4 showing basic principles and analytical studies have been studied. In other words, chiral sensing systems for the target analytes have been studied at the laboratory scale. Future work needs to be performed for the commercial availability of the proof-of-concept models constructed in the reported studies above. It can be suggested that smart-phone assisted on-site detection approach can be useful for optical detection or small portable bioelectronic devices would be highly desired for electrochemical detection for these applications. Besides, shedding light on the mechanism behind the chiral interaction between chiral pharmaceuticals and chiral nanomaterials, which is a missing point in the literature, may open new insights into pharmaceuticals and biomedical analysis research areas. As a perspective, the cavity-enhanced Raman spectroscopy might find application for the selective and sensitive detection of chiral pharmaceuticals.
